title: Columbus Ohio Notary Services — Detailed Business Plan

sections.title: Executive Summary
sections.contentMarkdown:
## Executive Summary
Columbus Ohio Notary Services is positioned as a Columbus-based hybrid notary business built around convenience, specialty service, digital delivery, and repeat client relationships.

- Strategy mode: Aggressive scale
- Startup cash outlay from model: $2,114
- Monthly fixed overhead: $540
- Year 1 revenue (base case): $36,645
- Year 1 EBITDA (base case): $20,538
- Break-even volume: 13.31 appointments/month
- Recommended capital target: $7,500

The base-case model projects Year 1 revenue of $36,645, EBITDA of $20,538, pre-tax net income of $20,305, and ending Year 1 cash of $18,424. By Year 3, the model reaches projected revenue of $51,621, EBITDA of $30,936, and ending cash above $52,200. Break-even occurs at roughly 13 appointments per month under the blended base-case ticket and cost structure.

The recommended capital target is $7,500, structured primarily around startup compliance costs, working capital reserve, local marketing, and workflow automation. A bootstrapped launch is feasible because the legally required startup spend is low; however, the lender/grant-ready plan also supports a small grant or microloan if the owner wants faster rollout or more working capital.

Source note: https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-147.08https://www.ohiosos.gov/notary/become-a-notary-in-ohio/application-processhttps://www.ohiosos.gov/notary/become-a-notary-in-ohio/resources-and-frequently-asked-questions

sections.title: Business Description
sections.contentMarkdown:
## Business Description
The business operates as a Columbus-based Ohio notary service with a phased revenue model that protects compliance first, then scales the highest-value lanes.

- The business will operate as a Columbus-based Ohio notary service with five primary revenue lanes. The plan is intentionally phased so that risk stays low in the first 90 days and capital is committed only after demand is validated.
- General mobile notary: everyday acknowledgments, jurats, affidavits, lease forms, school and employment forms.
- Specialty mobile notary: hospital, hospice, nursing home, bedside, title, estate-support, and difficult-location work.
- Remote online notarization (RON): higher-margin digital appointments with no drive time.
- Loan signing: selective real-estate package support after operating discipline and equipment are in place.
- Recurring B2B visits: law firms, healthcare administrators, HR/compliance teams, property managers, and other repeat-document clients.
- The business will not rely on unauthorized services. The plan assumes strict compliance with Ohio law: no legal advice unless separately licensed, no copy certification as a general service offering, no notarizing incomplete documents, and RON only after separate Ohio authorization is in place.
- Source note: https://www.ohiosos.gov/notary/become-a-notary-in-ohio/resources-and-frequently-asked-questionshttps://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/chapter-147

- Core principle: do not rely only on the statutory act fee.
- Revenue engine: combine lawful act fees with pre-agreed travel, specialty, urgency, package, and repeat-account economics where permitted.
- Process standard: the Mac app remains the operating system for intake, readiness, pricing discipline, route logic, and milestone tracking.

sections.title: Services and Revenue Lanes
sections.contentMarkdown:
## Services and Revenue Lanes
The business uses six tracked revenue lanes inside Notary OS so each lane can be turned on only after its prerequisites are satisfied.

- Employer / in-office: $15 revenue / $3 variable cost / $12 contribution per appointment. Best for practice volume, clean reps, and referral confidence.
- Mobile general: $45 revenue / $12 variable cost / $33 contribution per appointment. Best for fastest lawful local cash flow after commissioning.
- Specialty mobile: $85 revenue / $22 variable cost / $63 contribution per appointment. Best for urgent bedside/title/estate appointments with higher contribution per visit.
- RON: $40 revenue / $10 variable cost / $30 contribution per appointment. Best for margin protection by removing drive time and increasing scheduling flexibility.
- Loan signing: $125 revenue / $35 variable cost / $90 contribution per appointment. Best for highest contribution per appointment after premium workflow setup.
- Recurring B2B: $60 revenue / $15 variable cost / $45 contribution per appointment. Best for stable repeat revenue and lower dependence on one-off bookings.

Profit-max order for this build:
1. Employer / in-office for low-risk reps
2. Mobile general for fastest cash
3. Specialty mobile for best early margin
4. RON for best scale
5. Loan signing for highest late-stage margin
6. Recurring B2B for stability

sections.title: Market and Columbus Service-Area Strategy
sections.contentMarkdown:
## Market and Columbus Service-Area Strategy
Primary launch geography: Columbus, Franklin County, and nearby suburbs.

- Franklin County is a strong launch market for a notary business because it combines population density, employer concentration, solo-business activity, and real-estate transaction volume. The U.S. Census Bureau reports a Franklin County population of 1,323,807, 30,700 employer establishments, and 125,504 nonemployer establishments. Each of those figures supports recurring notary demand from residents, small businesses, healthcare facilities, and legal/compliance users.
- The housing market also supports notary-adjacent demand. Columbus REALTORS reported 29,626 closed sales in 2025 and more than $11.1 billion in gross residential sales across central Ohio. That activity supports title work, seller packages, refinance support, HELOC signings, estate planning follow-through, and related affidavit demand.
- Local pricing observations also show that competitors are monetizing travel, specialty locations, and signing packages rather than trying to live on the act fee. A Columbus-area mobile notary pricing page shows $5 general notarial acts, $35 travel within 20 miles, $55 RON, and loan signings starting at $110. Those examples do not set legal caps, but they validate the business model assumptions used in this plan.
- Source note: https://www.census.gov/quickfacts/fact/table/franklincountyohio/RHI425224https://data.census.gov/profile/Franklin_County%2C_Ohio?g=0500000US39049https://columbusrealtors.com/news/central-ohio-housing-report---december-2025https://centralohiomobilenotary.com/pricing
- 5. Revenue Model and Pricing Logic

Key market signals used in this plan:
- Franklin County population: 1323807 people (2020 Census profile / serviceable market context)
- Franklin County employer establishments: 30700 establishments (QuickFacts 2023)
- Franklin County nonemployer establishments: 125504 establishments (QuickFacts 2023)
- Central Ohio 2025 closed home sales: 29626 sales (Columbus REALTORS 2025 market activity)
- Central Ohio 2025 gross home sales: 11100000000 $ (Columbus REALTORS 2025 market activity)

Initial market focus:
- General public urgent documents
- Employer / internal volume
- Hospital / hospice / bedside signers
- Title / vehicle-related users
- Future law-firm, healthcare, HR, and property-manager accounts

sections.title: Operations and Launch Sequence
sections.contentMarkdown:
## Operations and Launch Sequence
The launch sequence is intentionally staged so the business becomes profitable without front-loading every cost at once.

1. File and activate the commission.
2. Finish the first-appointment launch kit.
3. Run low-risk employer / general mobile appointments.
4. Add specialty mobile after the intake and refusal SOPs are solid.
5. Add RON after separate Ohio authorization and tech setup.
6. Add loan signing after printer/scanner and package discipline are ready.
7. Add recurring B2B packages after repeatable delivery and invoicing are stable.

Operating-system support inside the app:
- The operating model is owner-led and workflow-driven. The notary app being built through Codex is strategically important because it converts the business from a loose service practice into a documented operating system. Intake, quote discipline, service-mode routing, appointment reminders, journal support, invoicing, and review follow-up will all sit inside the workflow stack.
- Intake and pre-screening: identify service type, signer readiness, ID status, document completeness, and whether the matter is suitable for mobile or RON.
- Quote generation: separate statutory act fees from travel, after-hours, specialty-location, and RON technology fees.
- Scheduling and reminders: reduce no-shows and document-readiness failures.
- Journal and audit support: support recordkeeping discipline, especially for RON.
- B2B account tracking: manage repeat clients, facilities, and law/title relationships over time.
- From a lender or grant perspective, the technology plan matters because it directly improves route efficiency, quote consistency, and conversion from inquiry to paid appointment.
- 7. Marketing and Sales Plan

sections.title: Compliance and Fee Controls
sections.contentMarkdown:
## Compliance and Fee Controls
Ohio fee controls and filing rules are encoded directly into the finance model so the business plan does not accidentally optimize around unlawful pricing.

- Traditional act fee cap: 5 $/act — Up to $5 for any notarial act that is not an online notarization. Effective April 3, 2025.
- RON act fee cap: 30 $/act — Online notarizations may be charged up to $30 per notarial act.
- RON technology fee cap: 10 $/session — A technology fee up to $10 may be charged for the online notarization system per session.
- Travel fee rule: Reasonable travel fee allowed rule — Travel must be agreed to in advance and is not charged per signature.
- Application upload set: Signature sample, BCI report, education/testing certificate requirements — These are the core upload requirements for filing the Ohio commission application.
- Education/testing proof window: 12 months — Education must be completed no later than 12 months before the application date.
- BCI background check fee: 22 $ — Required for filing but not included in the spreadsheet startup total.
- Ohio LLC Articles of Organization fee: 99 $ — Matches the business-plan spreadsheet LLC filing assumption.

Guardrails:
- Do not treat travel as a per-signature fee.
- Do not perform RON until the separate Ohio authorization is active.
- Do not use a notary seal for I-9 authorized representative work.
- Do not monetize prohibited or unauthorized services.

sections.title: Financial Model and Scenario Analysis
sections.contentMarkdown:
## Financial Model and Scenario Analysis
Base-case financial targets:
- Revenue: $36,645
- Variable cost: $9,627
- Contribution: $27,018
- EBITDA: $20,538
- Fixed monthly overhead: $540
- Break-even volume: 13.31 appointments/month

Startup and launch spend:
- Ohio notary education/testing: $130 (file_now; included in model)
- Ohio commission filing: $15 (file_now; included in model)
- BCI background check: $22 (file_now; official add-on / not in model total)
- Seal, journal, supplies: $150 (before_first_paid_appointment; included in model)
- Ohio LLC filing: $99 (before_mobile_launch; included in model)
- Website / domain / brand setup: $300 (before_mobile_launch; included in model)
- Phone / scheduling / software setup: $150 (before_mobile_launch; included in model)
- E&O insurance: $300 (before_first_paid_appointment; included in model)
- RON education/testing: $250 (before_ron_launch; included in model)
- RON filing: $20 (before_ron_launch; included in model)
- E-seal / digital certificate / RON setup: $250 (before_ron_launch; included in model)
- Printer / scanner / signing supplies: $450 (before_signing_agent_launch; included in model)

Monthly overhead:
- Software / scheduling / cloud: $65/month
- Phone / connectivity: $60/month
- Insurance: $25/month
- Marketing / reviews / local SEO: $200/month
- Office / admin / supplies: $75/month
- App workflow tools / automations: $75/month
- Contingency / misc.: $40/month

Scenario view:
- Conservative: $29,316 revenue / $15,134.40 EBITDA / 13.31 break-even appointments per month
- Base: $36,645 revenue / $20,538 EBITDA / 13.31 break-even appointments per month
- Growth: $45,806.25 revenue / $27,292.50 EBITDA / 13.31 break-even appointments per month

sections.title: Capital Plan and Use of Funds
sections.contentMarkdown:
## Capital Plan and Use of Funds
Recommended capital target: $7,500

Use of funds:
- Startup cash outlay: $2,114 — Commission, RON, entity, tools, printer, supplies
- Working capital reserve: $2,000 — Covers ramp period and no-show / timing volatility
- Initial marketing and reviews budget: $1,500 — Google Business Profile, local SEO, reviews, flyers
- App / workflow automation build: $1,000 — Codex-powered intake, reminders, invoicing workflow
- Contingency buffer: $886 — Brings recommended total target to $7,500

Suggested funding structure:
- Owner equity / bootstrapped cash: $2,500 — Retains control and aligns with SBA self-funding guidance
- Small grant / local program: $2,000 — Reduces repayment burden if available
- Microloan / SBA-backed or community loan: $3,000 — Completes capital stack without overleveraging

Funding approach ladder:
- Bootstrapping strategy: Before proof of demand / $2k-$3.5k — Use personal savings and current-job cash flow to control risk and retain control.
- Feasibility plan: First 90 days / $0-$2k incremental — Pilot the business, validate demand, and gather KPI evidence before borrowing.
- Startup plan: Lender / grant application / $5k-$10k — Present startup costs, 12-month cash flow, and ROI path to outside capital.
- Expansion / growth plan: After repeat demand appears / $7.5k-$20k — Scale marketing, RON capacity, equipment, and B2B outreach.

sections.title: Marketing and Client Acquisition
sections.contentMarkdown:
## Marketing and Client Acquisition
Early marketing should stay conversion-focused rather than vanity-focused.

- The first marketing phase is local and practical rather than brand-heavy. Columbus demand is likely to come from search, referrals, urgent need, and convenience, not from broad awareness advertising. Early spend should therefore go to trust assets and conversion assets rather than vanity branding.
- Google Business Profile, local citations, and review pipeline.
- Simple conversion website with clear pricing disclosures and service-area positioning.
- Direct outreach to assisted-living, hospice, elder-law, HR, and title-adjacent contacts.
- Employer-driven referrals and overflow demand from existing workplace experience.
- 8. Financial Plan

Priority channels:
- Google Business Profile and reviews
- Simple pricing-forward website
- Local SEO and citations
- Direct outreach to elder-care, hospice, HR, property-management, and title-adjacent contacts
- Repeat-account follow-up once the first 30 paid appointments are complete

sections.title: Milestones, Risks, and Next-Step Execution Plan
sections.contentMarkdown:
## Milestones, Risks, and Next-Step Execution Plan
Top operating risks:
- Compliance risk: managed through strict screening, app-based process controls, and avoiding prohibited services.
- Pricing risk: managed by separating legal act fees from travel and convenience charges and by focusing on blended-ticket niches.
- Demand risk: managed through a phased launch, employer-driven practice volume, and a 90-day feasibility pilot.
- Time-fragmentation risk: managed through service-area limits, appointment batching, and automated reminders.
- Working-capital risk: managed through a $2,000 reserve and optional microloan/grant layer.

Milestones:
- Timing
- Milestone
- Success indicator
- 0-30 days
- Complete commission / supplies / branding / intake
- Business is legally ready and operational
- 31-90 days
- Validate local mobile demand
- 30+ paid appointments and profitable blended ticket
- 90-180 days
- Launch RON and specialty niches
- Higher-margin revenue share increases
- 180-365 days
- Add B2B and selected loan signings
- Repeat-account revenue and premium appointments

Immediate next actions:
1. File the Ohio commission application.
2. Finish the first-appointment launch kit.
3. Activate mobile general as the first fast-cash lane.
4. Track margin by lane, route, and overhead weekly.
5. Unlock specialty mobile, then RON, then premium work in sequence.

2. Finish the first-appointment launch kit.
3. Activate mobile general as the first fast-cash lane.
4. Track margin by lane, route, and overhead weekly.
5. Unlock specialty mobile, then RON, then premium work in sequence.

# Columbus Ohio Notary Services — One-Page Financial Summary

## Core numbers
- Startup cash outlay: $2,114
- Required cash now: $595
- Monthly fixed overhead: $540
- Year 1 revenue: $36,645
- Year 1 EBITDA: $20,538
- Break-even: 13.31 appointments/month
- Recommended capital target: $7,500

## Best lane calls
- Fastest cash: Mobile general
- Highest margin: Loan signing
- Best scale: RON

## Do this now
- Launch mobile general first, then tilt into specialty mobile as soon as the SOPs are clean.
- Why: Aggressive scale works best when you create fast local cash first and then shift into higher-margin specialty visits.
- Unlocks: Specialty margin, RON scale, and later premium signings without burning cash too early.
